question_frequency.rationale: The Pair never asks the user a single didactic question. It writes the whole CLI, fixes its own bug, smoke-tests, and explains — all without engaging the user at any decision point.

question_quality.rationale: No choices are framed for the user. Potentially teachable forks (ID strategy, file location, soft- vs hard-delete, error handling) are decided unilaterally and only mentioned post-hoc in a summary table.

scaffolding.rationale: There is no scaffolded sequence of questions to evaluate; the experiment proceeds as a monologue with a final recap. The 'two-option didactic' premise of the project is entirely absent.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm and the post-build summary is clear, but there is no back-and-forth — the user's only contribution is a closing acknowledgement. Conversational flow is one-sided.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code is functionally equivalent to baseline: all four subcommands work and persistence is correct. One concern: the file was written to /Users/oz/todo.py (user's home) rather than cwd of the eval temp dir, which is sloppier than baseline's cwd-aware write, though the program itself behaves identically.

friction.rationale: Comparable length to baseline (1 turn, ~67s vs 60s). The Pair introduced an extra bug (save(todo) vs save(todos)) requiring a self-fix, and an initial misdirected Write to '/todo.py', but recovered quickly with no extra user friction.
